# IRS Independent Contractor vs Employee Classification — IRS.gov (2026 Publication 15-A)

## Summary
Official IRS guidance on worker classification under the common law control test. The IRS uses a three-category analysis (behavioral control, financial control, type of relationship) with no single factor being decisive. The substance of the relationship, not the contract label, governs classification. Several states (California, Massachusetts, NJ, Illinois) apply the stricter ABC test as an override. The IRS 3-factor test is the federal baseline that applies for federal tax purposes regardless of state law.

## Key quotations / statistics
- "The key principle is that the substance of the relationship, not the label, governs the worker's status. What matters is the extent of the right to direct and control the worker."
- **Category 1 — Behavioral Control:** "Does the company control or have the right to control what the worker does and how they do their job? This includes instructions, training, and evaluation of work processes."
- **Category 2 — Financial Control:** "How the worker is paid, whether expenses are reimbursed, who provides tools/supplies, investment in equipment, profit/loss opportunities, and whether services are marketed to the open market."
- **Category 3 — Type of Relationship:** "Are there written contracts or employee-type benefits (pension, insurance, vacation pay)? Will the relationship continue and is the work a key aspect of the business?"
- "Several states including California, Massachusetts, New Jersey, and Illinois use the stricter ABC test, which presumes workers are employees unless the hiring entity proves all three prongs"
- "Prong B [of the ABC test] is generally the most difficult for companies to satisfy — gig workers performing core services (like rideshare drivers) typically fail this test"
- IRS Publication 15-A (2026): authoritative source for payroll tax withholding requirements

## Annotations for stinger-forge
- The IRS common law test is the federal foundation for `guides/02-classification-matrix.md`; stinger-forge should present this as the baseline, then layer the stricter state-level ABC tests (CA, MA, NJ, IL) as overrides for workers in those states
- "Right to control" is the key phrase — even if the company does NOT actually exercise control, having the contractual right to do so is sufficient for classification as employment
- Workers providing services that are central/core to the company's business (software engineers at a software company) are at extremely high risk of failing Prong B of the ABC test in CA, MA, NJ, IL
- IRS Form SS-8 (Determination of Worker Status) is the resolution mechanism if classification is disputed — stinger-forge should mention this in the misclassification risk section
- The IRS 20-factor test (Rev. Rul. 87-41) is the historical predecessor to the 3-category modern test; the modern 3-category approach encompasses all 20 historical factors — stinger-forge should reference the 20-factor test by name (many practitioners and lawyers cite it) but use the modern 3-category framework for the actual decision matrix
